title: "Fact Checking" ChatGPT
summary: SNLI causality analysis of ChatGPT responses.
subtitle: Built a lightweight fact-checking pipeline that uses SNLI-trained models to probe whether ChatGPT outputs are causally consistent with verified reference facts.

sections.title: Summary
sections.body: An experiment in automated fact-checking: can we use Natural Language Inference models (trained on SNLI/MultiNLI) to detect when a language model's output contradicts established facts? This project builds a pipeline that compares ChatGPT responses against a curated reference set and produces an entailment-based consistency score.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Fine-tuned a DeBERTa-v3-large model on SNLI + MultiNLI for three-way classification (entailment, contradiction, neutral). Fed pairs of (ChatGPT response, reference fact) through the classifier. Aggregated per-response contradiction rates to produce a "fact-checking score" between 0 (fully contradictory) and 1 (fully consistent).

sections.title: Results & Interpretation
sections.body:
Across 500 evaluated responses, the pipeline identified a 12% contradiction rate on factual claims, with higher rates in medical and historical domains. The system correctly flagged 84% of manually-identified hallucinations.

Important caveat: NLI-based checking catches direct contradictions but misses subtle factual drift and omissions.

image.alt: AUPRC curve showing the fact-checking pipeline's performance across domains

sections.title: Generalization & Learnings
items: NLI models are surprisingly effective as lightweight fact-checkers for direct contradictions.
Domain-specific fine-tuning (medical NLI) significantly improves recall in specialized areas.
The approach generalizes to any generative model, not just ChatGPT.
Combining NLI scores with retrieval-augmented generation could close the gap on subtle errors.
